lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What, Lucius, ho!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I cannot, by the progress of the stars,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Give guess how near to day. Lucius, I say!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I would it were my fault to sleep so soundly.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: When, Lucius, when? awake, I say! what, Lucius!

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Call’d you, my lord?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Get me a taper in my study, Lucius:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: When it is lighted, come and call me here.

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: I will, my lord.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It must be by his death: and for my part,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I know no personal cause to spurn at him,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But for the general. He would be crown’d:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: How that might change his nature, there’s the question.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It is the bright day that brings forth the adder;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And that craves wary walking. Crown him?—that;—

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And then, I grant, we put a sting in him,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That at his will he may do danger with.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The abuse of greatness is, when it disjoins

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Remorse from power: and, to speak truth of Caesar,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I have not known when his affections sway’d

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: More than his reason. But ’tis a common proof,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That lowliness is young ambition’s ladder,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Whereto the climber-upward turns his face;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But when he once attains the upmost round,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He then unto the ladder turns his back,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Looks in the clouds, scorning the base degrees

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: By which he did ascend. So Caesar may.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Then, lest he may, prevent. And, since the quarrel

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Will bear no colour for the thing he is,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Fashion it thus; that what he is, augmented,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Would run to these and these extremities:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And therefore think him as a serpent’s egg

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Which, hatch’d, would, as his kind, grow mischievous,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And kill him in the shell.

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: The taper burneth in your closet, sir.

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Searching the window for a flint, I found

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: This paper, thus seal’d up; and, I am sure,

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: It did not lie there when I went to bed. Gives him the letter.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Get you to bed again; it is not day.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Is not to-morrow, boy, the ides of March?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: I know not, sir.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Look in the calendar, and bring me word.

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: I will, sir.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The exhalations whizzing in the air

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Give so much light that I may read by them. Opens the letter and reads.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: “Brutus, thou sleep’st: awake, and see thyself.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Shall Rome, etc. Speak, strike, redress!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Brutus, thou sleep’st: awake!”

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Such instigations have been often dropp’d

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Where I have took them up.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: “Shall Rome, etc.” Thus must I piece it out:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Shall Rome stand under one man’s awe? What, Rome?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: My ancestors did from the streets of Rome

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The Tarquin drive, when he was call’d a king.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: “Speak, strike, redress!” Am I entreated

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To speak and strike? O Rome, I make thee promise:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: If the redress will follow, thou receivest

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Thy full petition at the hand of Brutus!

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Sir, March is wasted fourteen days.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: ’Tis good. Go to the gate; somebody knocks.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Since Cassius first did whet me against Caesar,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I have not slept.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Between the acting of a dreadful thing

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And the first motion, all the interim is

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Like a phantasma, or a hideous dream:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The Genius and the mortal instruments

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Are then in council; and the state of man,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Like to a little kingdom, suffers then

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The nature of an insurrection.

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Sir, ’tis your brother Cassius at the door,

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Who doth desire to see you.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Is he alone?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: No, sir, there are moe with him.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Do you know them?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: No, sir; their hats are pluck’d about their ears,

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: And half their faces buried in their cloaks,

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: That by no means I may discover them

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: By any mark of favour.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let ’em enter.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: They are the faction. O conspiracy,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Shamest thou to show thy dangerous brow by night,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: When evils are most free? O, then by day

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Where wilt thou find a cavern dark enough

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To mask thy monstrous visage? Seek none, conspiracy;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Hide it in smiles and affability:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For if thou path, thy native semblance on,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Not Erebus itself were dim enough

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To hide thee from prevention.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I think we are too bold upon your rest:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Good morrow, Brutus; do we trouble you?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I have been up this hour, awake all night.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Know I these men that come along with you?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Yes, every man of them, and no man here

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But honours you; and everyone doth wish

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You had but that opinion of yourself

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Which every noble Roman bears of you.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This is Trebonius.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He is welcome hither.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This, Decius Brutus.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He is welcome too.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This, Casca; this, Cinna; and this, Metellus Cimber.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: They are all welcome.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What watchful cares do interpose themselves

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Betwixt your eyes and night?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Shall I entreat a word? Brutus and Cassius whisper.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Here lies the east: doth not the day break here?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: O, pardon, sir, it doth; and yon gray lines

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: That fret the clouds are messengers of day.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: You shall confess that you are both deceived.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Here, as I point my sword, the sun arises,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Which is a great way growing on the south,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Weighing the youthful season of the year.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Some two months hence up higher toward the north

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: He first presents his fire; and the high east

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Stands, as the Capitol, directly here.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Give me your hands all over, one by one.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And let us swear our resolution.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: No, not an oath: if not the face of men,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The sufferance of our souls, the time’s abuse—

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: If these be motives weak, break off betimes,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And every man hence to his idle bed;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: So let high-sighted tyranny range on,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Till each man drop by lottery. But if these,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: As I am sure they do, bear fire enough

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To kindle cowards and to steel with valour

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The melting spirits of women, then, countrymen,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What need we any spur but our own cause,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To prick us to redress? what other bond

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Than secret Romans, that have spoke the word,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And will not palter? and what other oath

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Than honesty to honesty engaged,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That this shall be, or we will fall for it?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Swear priests and cowards and men cautelous,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Old feeble carrions and such suffering souls

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That welcome wrongs; unto bad causes swear

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Such creatures as men doubt; but do not stain

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The even virtue of our enterprise,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Nor the insuppressive mettle of our spirits,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To think that or our cause or our performance

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Did need an oath; when every drop of blood

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That every Roman bears, and nobly bears,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Is guilty of a several bastardy,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: If he do break the smallest particle

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Of any promise that hath pass’d from him.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But what of Cicero? shall we sound him?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I think he will stand very strong with us.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Let us not leave him out.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: No, by no means.

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: O, let us have him, for his silver hairs

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: Will purchase us a good opinion

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: And buy men’s voices to commend our deeds:

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: It shall be said, his judgment ruled our hands;

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: Our youths and wildness shall no whit appear,

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: But all be buried in his gravity.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: O, name him not: let us not break with him;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For he will never follow anything

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That other men begin.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Then leave him out.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Indeed he is not fit.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Shall no man else be touch’d but only Caesar?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Decius, well urged: I think it is not meet,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Mark Antony, so well beloved of Caesar,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Should outlive Caesar: we shall find of him

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: A shrewd contriver; and, you know, his means,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: If he improve them, may well stretch so far

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: As to annoy us all: which to prevent,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Let Antony and Caesar fall together.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Our course will seem too bloody, Caius Cassius,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To cut the head off and then hack the limbs,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Like wrath in death and envy afterwards;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For Antony is but a limb of Caesar:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let us be sacrificers, but not butchers, Caius.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: We all stand up against the spirit of Caesar;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And in the spirit of men there is no blood:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: O, that we then could come by Caesar’s spirit,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And not dismember Caesar! But, alas,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Caesar must bleed for it! And, gentle friends,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let’s kill him boldly, but not wrathfully;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let’s carve him as a dish fit for the gods,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Not hew him as a carcass fit for hounds:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And let our hearts, as subtle masters do,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Stir up their servants to an act of rage,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And after seem to chide ’em. This shall make

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Our purpose necessary and not envious:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Which so appearing to the common eyes,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: We shall be call’d purgers, not murderers.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And for Mark Antony, think not of him;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For he can do no more than Caesar’s arm

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: When Caesar’s head is off.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Yet I fear him;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For in the ingrafted love he bears to Caesar—

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Alas, good Cassius, do not think of him:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: If he love Caesar, all that he can do

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Is to himself, take thought and die for Caesar:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And that were much he should; for he is given

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To sports, to wildness and much company.

lines.speaker: TREBONIUS
lines.text: There is no fear in him: let him not die;

lines.speaker: TREBONIUS
lines.text: For he will live, and laugh at this hereafter. Clock strikes.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Peace! count the clock.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The clock hath stricken three.

lines.speaker: TREBONIUS
lines.text: ’Tis time to part.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But it is doubtful yet,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Whether Caesar will come forth to-day, or no;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For he is superstitious grown of late,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Quite from the main opinion he held once

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Of fantasy, of dreams and ceremonies:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: It may be, these apparent prodigies,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The unaccustom’d terror of this night,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And the persuasion of his augurers,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: May hold him from the Capitol to-day.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Never fear that: if he be so resolved,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: I can o’ersway him; for he loves to hear

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: That unicorns may be betray’d with trees,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: And bears with glasses, elephants with holes,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Lions with toils and men with flatterers;

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: But when I tell him he hates flatterers,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: He says he does, being then most flattered.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Let me work;

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: For I can give his humour the true bent,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: And I will bring him to the Capitol.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Nay, we will all of us be there to fetch him.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: By the eighth hour: is that the uttermost?

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Be that the uttermost, and fail not then.

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: Caius Ligarius doth bear Caesar hard,

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: Who rated him for speaking well of Pompey:

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: I wonder none of you have thought of him.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Now, good Metellus, go along by him:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He loves me well, and I have given him reasons;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Send him but hither, and I’ll fashion him.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The morning comes upon’s: we’ll leave you, Brutus.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And, friends, disperse yourselves; but all remember

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: What you have said, and show yourselves true Romans.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Good gentlemen, look fresh and merrily;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let not our looks put on our purposes,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But bear it as our Roman actors do,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: With untired spirits and formal constancy:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And so good morrow to you everyone.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Boy! Lucius! Fast asleep? It is no matter;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Enjoy the honey-heavy dew of slumber:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Thou hast no figures nor no fantasies,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Which busy care draws in the brains of men;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Therefore thou sleep’st so sound.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Brutus, my lord!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Portia, what mean you? wherefore rise you now?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It is not for your health thus to commit

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Your weak condition to the raw cold morning.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Nor for yours neither. You’ve ungently, Brutus,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Stole from my bed: and yesternight, at supper,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: You suddenly arose, and walk’d about,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Musing and sighing, with your arms across,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: And when I ask’d you what the matter was,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: You stared upon me with ungentle looks;

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I urged you further; then you scratch’d your head,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: And too impatiently stamp’d with your foot;

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Yet I insisted, yet you answer’d not,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: But, with an angry wafture of your hand,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Gave sign for me to leave you: so I did;

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Fearing to strengthen that impatience

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Which seem’d too much enkindled, and withal

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Hoping it was but an effect of humour,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Which sometime hath his hour with every man.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: It will not let you eat, nor talk, nor sleep,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: And could it work so much upon your shape

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: As it hath much prevail’d on your condition,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I should not know you, Brutus. Dear my lord,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Make me acquainted with your cause of grief.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I am not well in health, and that is all.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Brutus is wise, and, were he not in health,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: He would embrace the means to come by it.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Why, so I do. Good Portia, go to bed.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Is Brutus sick? and is it physical

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: To walk unbraced and suck up the humours

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Of the dank morning? What, is Brutus sick,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: And will he steal out of his wholesome bed,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: To dare the vile contagion of the night

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: And tempt the rheumy and unpurged air

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: To add unto his sickness? No, my Brutus;

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: You have some sick offence within your mind,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Which, by the right and virtue of my place,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I ought to know of: and, upon my knees,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I charm you, by my once-commended beauty,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: By all your vows of love and that great vow

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Which did incorporate and make us one,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: That you unfold to me, yourself, your half,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Why you are heavy, and what men to-night

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Have had to resort to you: for here have been

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Some six or seven, who did hide their faces

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Even from darkness.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Kneel not, gentle Portia.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I should not need, if you were gentle Brutus.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Within the bond of marriage, tell me, Brutus,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Is it excepted I should know no secrets

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: That appertain to you? Am I yourself

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: But, as it were, in sort or limitation,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: To keep with you at meals, comfort your bed,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: And talk to you sometimes? Dwell I but in the suburbs

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Of your good pleasure? If it be no more,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Portia is Brutus’ harlot, not his wife.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: You are my true and honourable wife,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: As dear to me as are the ruddy drops

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That visit my sad heart.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: If this were true, then should I know this secret.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I grant I am a woman; but withal

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: A woman that Lord Brutus took to wife:

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I grant I am a woman; but withal

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: A woman well-reputed, Cato’s daughter.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Think you I am no stronger than my sex,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Being so father’d and so husbanded?

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Tell me your counsels, I will not disclose ’em:

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I have made strong proof of my constancy,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Giving myself a voluntary wound

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Here, in the thigh: can I bear that with patience,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: And not my husband’s secrets?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: O ye gods,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Render me worthy of this noble wife!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Hark, hark! one knocks: Portia, go in awhile;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And by and by thy bosom shall partake

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The secrets of my heart.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: All my engagements I will construe to thee,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: All the charactery of my sad brows:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Leave me with haste.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Lucius, who’s that knocks?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Here is a sick man that would speak with you.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Caius Ligarius, that Metellus spake of.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Boy, stand aside. Caius Ligarius! how?

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: Vouchsafe good morrow from a feeble tongue.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: O, what a time have you chose out, brave Caius,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To wear a kerchief! Would you were not sick!

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: I am not sick, if Brutus have in hand

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: Any exploit worthy the name of honour.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Such an exploit have I in hand, Ligarius,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Had you a healthful ear to hear of it.

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: By all the gods that Romans bow before,

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: I here discard my sickness! Soul of Rome!

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: Brave son, derived from honourable loins!

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: Thou, like an exorcist, hast conjured up

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: My mortified spirit. Now bid me run,

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: And I will strive with things impossible;

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: Yea, get the better of them. What’s to do?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: A piece of work that will make sick men whole.

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: But are not some whole that we must make sick?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That must we also. What it is, my Caius,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I shall unfold to thee, as we are going

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To whom it must be done.

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: Set on your foot,

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: And with a heart new-fired I follow you,

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: To do I know not what: but it sufficeth

lines.speaker: LIGARIUS
lines.text: That Brutus leads me on.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Follow me, then.

stage_directions.text: Enter the conspirators, Cassius, Casca, Decius, Cinna, Metellus Cimber, and Trebonius.

glosses.definition: slender candle
kick at; lash out against
for the common good; for the general public

glosses.phrase: bright day that brings forth the adder
glosses.definition: warm weather brings out the snake — Brutus arguing that Caesar's crown would activate latent tyranny
demands that we walk carefully
separates pity/conscience from political authority
commonly-demonstrated truth
humility is the rung by which a rising ambitious man climbs — a Plutarchan proverb
topmost rung of the ladder
lower rungs; humble beginnings
the turning-point of Brutus's argument — he reasons from what Caesar 'may' do, not from what he has done; a logical weakness
to prevent what he might do — Brutus convinces himself to act on hypothetical, not actual, cause
cause for dispute; grievance
frame the argument this way
a snake-egg — kill the threat before it hatches into something deadly
private study; small inner room
a piece of flint for striking sparks — the period's means of lighting a candle
March 15 — the assassination date
meteors; fiery vapours believed to rise from the earth and ignite in the sky
the forged letter's command — speak out, strike (kill Caesar), set things right
Tarquinius Superbus, the last Roman king; expelled in 509 BCE by Lucius Junius Brutus (Marcus's legendary ancestor); his expulsion founded the Roman Republic
fourteen days of March have passed — it is therefore March 15, the Ides
sharpened my resolve against (as one whets a blade)
phantasm; nightmarish hallucination
guiding spirit and the physical body/faculties — Neoplatonic psychology; the interval between impulse and action
the self conceived as a miniature polity — the body-politic metaphor turned inward
rebellion within a state — here, a rebellion within the self
more (archaic)
pulled down over their ears — hats pulled low to disguise identity
distinguishing feature of the face
when wickedness is least constrained — i.e. by night
in Greek mythology, the primordial deity of darkness; the dark region of the underworld between earth and Hades
being forestalled; being stopped before acting
anxious concerns that keep one awake
streak or etch the clouds (as a carved frieze)
the patient endurance of our spirits — what we have endured
stop now, in good time
soaring, far-ranging tyranny — like a high-flying hawk surveying its prey
fall one by one as chosen by chance — under tyrannical proscription
prevaricate; equivocate; break faith
cautious; wary; deceitful
decaying bodies; metaphor for worn-out men
unconquerable spirit (mettle = essential character; pun on metal)
a separate act of bastardry — each Roman's blood is dishonoured if he breaks his word
test his willingness; probe his opinion
grey hair — sign of venerable age
dignity; solemn authority of age
tell him (the secret); open the matter to him
well argued; a good point
cunning schemer
injure or harm us all (stronger than modern 'annoy')

glosses.phrase: Like wrath in death and envy afterwards
glosses.definition: as though killing Caesar in rage and then dismembering his corpse in malice
we perform a religious sacrifice, not a slaughter — Brutus's self-deception about the assassination's moral character

glosses.phrase: carve him as a dish fit for the gods
glosses.definition: slay Caesar as a ceremonial offering — elevated, sanctified violence
purifiers; those who cleanse the body politic (a surgical metaphor)
deeply rooted; grafted in (as a tree-graft)
mechanical clocks are anachronistic — they didn't exist until c. 1300 CE, some 1,350 years after the play's setting. Shakespeare's Rome is deliberately English-inflected
become superstitious
imagination, dreams, and religious rituals
Roman priests who divined the will of the gods by reading bird-flight, entrails, and other signs

glosses.phrase: unicorns may be betray'd with trees
glosses.definition: unicorns were said to be caught by provoking them to charge at a tree, where their horn stuck fast — a fable Decius claims Caesar enjoys
nets; snares

glosses.phrase: give his humour the true bent
glosses.definition: bend his mood in the right direction — manipulate him correctly
the latest (time); the outside limit

glosses.phrase: Caius Ligarius doth bear Caesar hard
glosses.definition: Caius Ligarius holds a grudge against Caesar
scolded him; rebuked him
shape him to our purpose; win him over
reveal our intentions by our faces
the measured self-possession of a stage actor — Brutus invokes Roman theatre as model for political dissembling
heavy sweet dew of sleep — Brutus's envy of the boy's untroubled sleep
mental images; imaginings
wave (of the hand)
symptom of a bodily humour — a temporary mood tied to physiology
medicinal; health-giving
with doublet unlaced and open at the chest — the Renaissance garment again, not a Roman toga
damp, disease-carrying air before the sun has cleaned it
entreat; solemnly adjure (not modern 'charm')
united us into one body — the marriage vow
the outskirts of a city — proverbially the location of brothels in Elizabethan London; Portia accuses Brutus of relegating her to the margins of his life
prostitute; whore
drops of red blood
Portia is the daughter of Marcus Porcius Cato the Younger, the famous Roman Stoic philosopher and Caesar's chief senatorial opponent; her Stoic lineage is her claim to fortitude
self-inflicted wound — Portia stabs her thigh (Plutarch, *Life of Brutus*) to prove her ability to bear pain and thus her husband's secrets
the wound Portia inflicted on her own thigh; a detail directly from North's Plutarch
what is written; the characters (letters) legible on a face
be pleased to grant; deign to accept
a cloth tied around the head — sign of illness in the period
noble ancestry; descended from honourable stock
one who raises spirits — not a modern exorcist (who expels), but a summoner
deadened, subdued spirit

annotations.title: 'It must be by his death' — the soliloquy that tries to reason its way into murder
annotations.body:
Brutus's opening soliloquy is an extended attempt to justify an assassination on hypothetical grounds. The logical seam runs through the phrase 'So Caesar may. / Then, lest he may, prevent.' Brutus has just finished noting, honestly, that Caesar's *actual* conduct has not yet separated reason from affection ('I have not known when his affections sway'd / More than his reason'). He then reasons: if Caesar *might* change once crowned, we must prevent what Caesar might do. The move from observed behaviour to speculative character to justified violence is extraordinary — and Shakespeare means for us to notice it.

This is Brutus's characterological limit in the play: a capacity for high abstract principle disconnected from the evidence of his actual friend's actual conduct. He cannot produce a charge against Caesar; he can only produce a charge against what Caesar might become. The serpent's-egg metaphor he lands on — kill it before it hatches — does the argument's last step: we kill not for what a person has done, but for what they could do, which is a principle that will justify any preemptive violence.

This is the play's most damning moment about Brutus's judgment. Ben Jonson, no friend of the play, identified the political danger in his 1640 *Timber*: 'his honest, friendly, and manly mixture' is precisely what made his philosophical weakness so hard to see.
sources: Harold Bloom, *Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human* (1998)

annotations.title: The little kingdom of the self
annotations.body:
Brutus's second soliloquy, after the conspirators knock, makes the analogy explicit: 'the state of man, / Like to a little kingdom, suffers then / The nature of an insurrection.' The body-politic metaphor, classical in origin (Livy's fable of the belly and the limbs) but radically internalised, is Brutus's key image of himself. The decision to kill Caesar is figured as an insurrection inside him — not against Caesar but against Brutus's own self-government.

The metaphor is double-edged in ways Brutus doesn't see. If the polity is sick, the doctor kills and purges; but the metaphor treats the assassination as a cure for Brutus's internal disease, not Rome's. By the time he ends the speech — 'Hide it in smiles and affability' — he has turned conspiracy itself into a state of mind. The conspiracy is a mental condition before it is a political act.
sources: E. M. W. Tillyard, *The Elizabethan World Picture* (1943)

annotations.title: 'No oath' — Brutus refuses to swear the conspiracy
annotations.body:
Cassius proposes a binding oath; Brutus refuses. The refusal is a major act of leadership and a subtler act of self-deception. The stated reason — Roman honesty needs no oath — is a Stoic ideal that the conspiracy will immediately fail to live up to (Cassius's bribery in 4.3; Casca striking from behind). Brutus insists on the purity of the act at the moment he is organising it.

The refusal also has a structural effect: it makes Brutus the conscience of the conspiracy, which is what Cassius wanted. The no-oath moment positions Brutus, not Cassius, as the moral centre. The consequences follow: Brutus overrules Cassius on sparing Antony (L150–189), on letting Antony speak at the funeral (3.1 L228–248), and on marching to Philippi (4.3 L192–221). Each of Brutus's decisions, morally principled, is tactically disastrous. Shakespeare makes this the play's quiet running pattern: the integrity that recommends Brutus to the conspiracy is what loses the conspiracy the war.

annotations.title: Sparing Antony: Brutus's first catastrophic decision
annotations.body:
Cassius proposes killing Antony with Caesar. Brutus refuses on the ground that it would make them appear 'butchers' rather than 'sacrificers' (L166). The distinction is specifically religious: Brutus imagines the assassination as ritual, a sanctified ceremony, and a second killing would desecrate the rite. 'Let's kill him boldly, but not wrathfully; / Let's carve him as a dish fit for the gods, / Not hew him as a carcass fit for hounds.'

This is the play's single most consequential tactical error. Antony will use his survival to deliver the funeral oration of 3.2, raise an army, and win Philippi. Shakespeare has Cassius — the shrewder politician — see this clearly: 'Yet I fear him.' Brutus dismisses the fear. The Stoic ideologue outvotes the pragmatist, and Rome loses its Republic.

Plutarch's *Life of Brutus* §18 records this debate as historical. Shakespeare's dramatic contribution is the ritual-vs-butchery framing, which shows us exactly what Brutus thinks he is doing: not simply refusing a second death, but preserving the *aesthetic* integrity of the first.

annotations.title: 'The clock hath stricken three': the play's most famous anachronism
annotations.body:
Cassius's line is probably the most widely cited anachronism in Shakespeare. Mechanical clocks capable of striking the hour did not exist until roughly 1300 CE, some 1,350 years after the play's setting. Public striking clocks became common in European cities only in the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries; a first-century BCE Roman would have told time by sundials, water-clocks, and cockcrow.

This is deliberate. Shakespeare knew — his sources told him — that Romans did not have clocks. The clock is here because the scene needs an auditory beat to close off the conspiratorial discussion and because the English audience needed a time-signal they recognised. The play commits this particular anachronism four times (2.1.191, 2.1.192, 2.2.115, 4.3.268 by implication in the book-reading).

The critical tradition has generally treated the anachronisms as Shakespeare's problem. The better reading is the reverse: the anachronisms are one of the tools by which the play makes Roman politics legible as English politics. Hearing a clock strike three makes the conspirators English gentlemen meeting before dawn, which is precisely the imaginative transport Shakespeare needs. The play's political argument — about the line between lawful sovereignty and tyranny — lands harder on an Elizabethan audience when the stage-Rome looks and sounds like London.
sources: Andrew Gurr, 'Elizabethan Time and Tempest Time', *SQ* 33 (1982)

annotations.title: Portia's wound: the scene that will mirror Calpurnia's
annotations.body:
Portia's plea and her wounded thigh is one of the play's two parallel wife-husband scenes; Calpurnia's in 2.2 will restage it from the other side. Portia invokes her lineage as Cato the Younger's daughter — the Republic's most famous Stoic — and her marriage vow ('Within the bond of marriage') to claim a right to her husband's secrets. She has proved her constancy by a voluntary self-wounding, a detail taken directly from Plutarch's *Life of Brutus* §13.

Shakespeare builds the scene around the self-inflicted wound with care. Portia's claim is not that women in general can keep secrets; it is that a Stoic-reared, Cato-fathered, Brutus-married woman has trained herself into fortitude. The claim is conditional, identity-based, Roman. It is also one of the very few Shakespeare scenes in which a wife actually wins the argument.

But she wins it only partly. Brutus promises to tell her, then is interrupted by Ligarius's entrance and never does. Shakespeare pairs this near-confidence with Calpurnia's outright dismissal in 2.2. Both women are dismissed. Both husbands die within days. The play makes a structural, gendered critique by parallel, not by speech: each scene resonates with the other, and each dismissal licenses the next catastrophe.

trials.prompt: Brutus's 'So Caesar may. / Then, lest he may, prevent' justifies killing Caesar on the grounds of:
options: What Caesar *might* become if crowned — a preemptive standard that Brutus extends from observed behaviour to speculative character

trials.prompt: When Brutus refuses Cassius's proposed oath, what does he achieve?
options: He positions himself — not Cassius — as the moral centre of the conspiracy, which lets him later overrule Cassius on sparing Antony, letting Antony speak, and marching to Philippi

trials.prompt: Brutus's argument for sparing Antony ('sacrificers, but not butchers') is:
options: Politically shrewd — Antony will be no threat
Religiously coherent but politically disastrous — Antony's funeral oration in 3.2 and rise at Philippi directly follow from this decision
A secret plan to kill Antony later
A deliberate anachronism — mechanical striking clocks didn't exist until c. 1300 CE, and the anachronism is one of several that make Shakespeare's Rome legible as English politics

trials.prompt: What does Portia do to prove she can keep her husband's secrets?
options: Inflicts a voluntary wound on her own thigh — a detail Shakespeare takes directly from North's Plutarch

trials.prompt: Portia and Calpurnia (2.1 / 2.2) function structurally as:
options: Minor characters with no dramatic weight
Mirror scenes — both wives plead with their husbands, both are dismissed, and both husbands die shortly after; a gendered critique by structural parallel
Subplots unrelated to the main plot

trials.prompt: Why does Brutus's 'little kingdom' image matter?
options: It figures the decision to kill Caesar as an insurrection inside Brutus himself — a body-politic metaphor turned inward, so that the conspiracy is first a state of mind
It refers to a children's toy

trials.prompt: What does Brutus's characteristic movement through this scene — from 'must be by his death' to refusing the oath to sparing Antony to dismissing Portia — establish?
options: A pattern: every principled stand Brutus takes has catastrophic tactical consequences; honour loses the war
